lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: How now, my lord, what say the citizens?

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Now, by the holy mother of our Lord,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: The citizens are mum and speak not a word.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Touch’d you the bastardy of Edward’s children?

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: I did; with his contract with Lady Lucy,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And his contract by deputy in France;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: The insatiate greediness of his desires,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And his enforcement of the city wives;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: His tyranny for trifles; his own bastardy,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: As being got, your father then in France,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: His resemblance, being not like the duke:

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Withal I did infer your lineaments,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Being the right idea of your father,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Both in your form and nobleness of mind;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Laid open all your victories in Scotland,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Your dicipline in war, wisdom in peace,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Your bounty, virtue, fair humility;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Indeed, left nothing fitting for the purpose

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Untouch’d, or slightly handled, in discourse:

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And when mine oratory grew to an end,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: I bid them that did love their country’s good

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Cry “God save Richard, England’s royal king!”

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Ah! and did they so?

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: No, so God help me, they spake not a word;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: But, like dumb statuas or breathing stones,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Gazed each on other, and look’d deadly pale.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Which when I saw, I reprehended them;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And ask’d the mayor what meant this wilful silence:

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: His answer was, the people were not wont

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: To be spoke to but by the recorder.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Then he was urged to tell my tale again,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: “Thus saith the duke, thus hath the duke inferr’d;”

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: But nothing spake in warrant from himself.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: When he had done, some followers of mine own,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: At the lower end of the hall, hurl’d up their caps,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And some ten voices cried “God save King Richard!”

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And thus I took the vantage of those few,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: “Thanks, gentle citizens and friends,” quoth I;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: “This general applause and loving shout

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Argues your wisdoms and your love to Richard:”

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And even here brake off, and came away.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: What tongueless blocks were they! would not they speak?

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: No, by my troth, my lord.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Will not the mayor then and his brethren come?

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: The mayor is here at hand: intend some fear;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Be not you spoke with, but by mighty suit:

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And look you get a prayer-book in your hand,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And stand betwixt two churchmen, good my lord;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: For on that ground I’ll build a holy descant:

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And be not easily won to our request:

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Play the maid’s part, still answer nay, and take it.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I go; and if you plead as well for them

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: As I can say nay to thee for myself,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: No doubt we’ll bring it to a happy issue.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Go, go, up to the leads; the lord mayor knocks.

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: My lord, he doth entreat your grace

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: To visit him to-morrow or next day:

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: He is within, with two right reverend fathers,

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: Divinely bent to meditation;

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: And no worldly suit would he be moved,

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: To draw him from his holy exercise.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Return, good Catesby, to thy lord again;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Tell him, myself, the mayor and citizens,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: In deep designs and matters of great moment,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: No less importing than our general good,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Are come to have some conference with his grace.

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: I’ll tell him what you say, my lord.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Ah, ha, my lord, this prince is not an Edward!

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: He is not lolling on a lewd day-bed,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: But on his knees at meditation;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Not dallying with a brace of courtezans,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: But meditating with two deep divines;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Not sleeping, to engross his idle body,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: But praying, to enrich his watchful soul:

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Happy were England, would this gracious prince

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Take on himself the sovereignty thereof:

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: But, sure, I fear, we shall ne’er win him to it.

lines.speaker: MAYOR
lines.text: Marry, God forbid his grace should say us nay!

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: I fear he will.

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: My lord,

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: He wonders to what end you have assembled

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: Such troops of citizens to speak with him,

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: His grace not being warn’d thereof before:

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: My lord, he fears you mean no good to him.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Sorry I am my noble cousin should

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Suspect me, that I mean no good to him:

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: By heaven, I come in perfect love to him;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And so once more return and tell his grace.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: When holy and devout religious men

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Are at their beads, ’tis hard to draw them thence,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: So sweet is zealous contemplation.

lines.speaker: MAYOR
lines.text: See, where he stands between two clergymen!

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Two props of virtue for a Christian prince,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: To stay him from the fall of vanity:

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And, see, a book of prayer in his hand,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: True ornaments to know a holy man.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Famous Plantagenet, most gracious prince,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Lend favourable ears to our request;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And pardon us the interruption

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Of thy devotion and right Christian zeal.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: My lord, there needs no such apology:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I rather do beseech you pardon me,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Who, earnest in the service of my God,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Neglect the visitation of my friends.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: But, leaving this, what is your grace’s pleasure?

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Even that, I hope, which pleaseth God above,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And all good men of this ungovern’d isle.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I do suspect I have done some offence

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: That seems disgracious in the city’s eyes,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And that you come to reprehend my ignorance.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: You have, my lord: would it might please your grace,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: At our entreaties, to amend that fault!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Else wherefore breathe I in a Christian land?

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Then know, it is your fault that you resign

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: The supreme seat, the throne majestical,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: The scepter’d office of your ancestors,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Your state of fortune and your due of birth,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: The lineal glory of your royal house,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: To the corruption of a blemished stock:

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Whilst, in the mildness of your sleepy thoughts,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Which here we waken to our country’s good,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: This noble isle doth want her proper limbs;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Her face defaced with scars of infamy,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Her royal stock graft with ignoble plants,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And almost shoulder’d in the swallowing gulf

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Of blind forgetfulness and dark oblivion.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Which to recure, we heartily solicit

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Your gracious self to take on you the charge

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And kingly government of this your land;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Not as protector, steward, substitute,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Or lowly factor for another’s gain;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: But as successively from blood to blood,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Your right of birth, your empery, your own.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: For this, consorted with the citizens,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Your very worshipful and loving friends,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And by their vehement instigation,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: In this just suit come I to move your grace.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I know not whether to depart in silence,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Or bitterly to speak in your reproof,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Best fitteth my degree or your condition:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: If not to answer, you might haply think

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Tongue-tied ambition, not replying, yielded

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: To bear the golden yoke of sovereignty,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Which fondly you would here impose on me;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: If to reprove you for this suit of yours,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: So season’d with your faithful love to me,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Then, on the other side, I check’d my friends.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Therefore, to speak, and to avoid the first,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And then, in speaking, not to incur the last,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Definitively thus I answer you.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Your love deserves my thanks; but my desert

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Unmeritable shuns your high request.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: First, if all obstacles were cut away,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And that my path were even to the crown,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: As my ripe revenue and due by birth;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Yet so much is my poverty of spirit,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: So mighty and so many my defects,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: As I had rather hide me from my greatness,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Being a bark to brook no mighty sea,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Than in my greatness covet to be hid,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And in the vapour of my glory smother’d.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: But, God be thank’d, there’s no need of me,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And much I need to help you, if need were;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: The royal tree hath left us royal fruit,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Which, mellow’d by the stealing hours of time,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Will well become the seat of majesty,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And make, no doubt, us happy by his reign.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: On him I lay what you would lay on me,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: The right and fortune of his happy stars;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Which God defend that I should wring from him!

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: My lord, this argues conscience in your grace;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: But the respects thereof are nice and trivial,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: All circumstances well considered.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: You say that Edward is your brother’s son:

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: So say we too, but not by Edward’s wife;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: For first he was contract to Lady Lucy—

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Your mother lives a witness to that vow—

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And afterward by substitute betroth’d

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: To Bona, sister to the King of France.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: These both put by, a poor petitioner,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: A care-crazed mother of a many children,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: A beauty-waning and distressed widow,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Even in the afternoon of her best days,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Made prize and purchase of his lustful eye,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Seduced the pitch and height of all his thoughts

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: To base declension and loathed bigamy:

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: By her, in his unlawful bed, he got

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: This Edward, whom our manners term the prince.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: More bitterly could I expostulate,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Save that, for reverence to some alive,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: I give a sparing limit to my tongue.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Then, good my lord, take to your royal self

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: This proffer’d benefit of dignity;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: If not to bless us and the land withal,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Yet to draw forth your noble ancestry

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: From the corruption of abusing times,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Unto a lineal true-derived course.

lines.speaker: MAYOR
lines.text: Do, good my lord, your citizens entreat you.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Refuse not, mighty lord, this proffer’d love.

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: O, make them joyful, grant their lawful suit!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Alas, why would you heap these cares on me?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I am unfit for state and majesty:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I do beseech you, take it not amiss;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I cannot nor I will not yield to you.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: If you refuse it—as, in love and zeal,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Loath to depose the child, your brother’s son;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: As well we know your tenderness of heart

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And gentle, kind, effeminate remorse,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Which we have noted in you to your kin,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And egally indeed to all estates—

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Yet whether you accept our suit or no,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Your brother’s son shall never reign our king;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: But we will plant some other in the throne,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: To the disgrace and downfall of your house:

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And in this resolution here we leave you.—

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Come, citizens: ’zounds! I’ll entreat no more.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: O, do not swear, my lord of Buckingham.

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: Call them again, my lord, and accept their suit.

lines.speaker: ANOTHER
lines.text: Do, good my lord, lest all the land do rue it.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Would you enforce me to a world of care?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Well, call them again. I am not made of stones,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: But penetrable to your kind entreats,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Albeit against my conscience and my soul.

lines.speaker: MAYOR
lines.text: God bless your grace! we see it, and will say it.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: In saying so, you shall but say the truth.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Then I salute you with this kingly title:

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Long live Richard, England’s royal king!

lines.speaker: MAYOR CITIZENS
lines.text: Amen.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: To-morrow will it please you to be crown’d?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Even when you please, since you will have it so.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: To-morrow, then, we will attend your grace:

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And so most joyfully we take our leave.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Come, let us to our holy task again.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Farewell, good cousin; farewell, gentle friends.

stage_directions.text: Enter Gloucester and Buckingham, at several doors.
Welcome my lord: I dance attendance here; I think the duke will not be spoke withal.
Here comes his servant: how now, Catesby, What says he?
How now, Catesby, what says your lord?
Enter Gloucester aloft, between two Bishops. Catesby returns.

Cousin of Buckingham, and you sage, grave men, Since you will buckle fortune on my back, To bear her burthen, whether I will or no, I must have patience to endure the load: But if black scandal or foul-faced reproach Attend the sequel of your imposition, Your mere enforcement shall acquittance me From all the impure blots and stains thereof; For God he knows, and you may partly see, How far I am from the desire thereof.

glosses.phrase: mum and speak not a word
glosses.definition: silent — the citizens' refusal to acclaim Richard is a political disaster Buckingham must spin
Elizabeth Lucy, Edward IV's mistress, claimed by some to have been pre-contracted to him — the pretext for the 'bastardy' charge against Edward's marriage
Edward's diplomatic marriage-contract to Bona of Savoy, never consummated
Edward's forcing of London citizens' wives — the charge of sexual predation
the claim that Edward IV himself was illegitimate — gossip revived from the Duke of York's long absences in France; Buckingham repeats it, slandering Richard's own mother
features, physical likeness
the true image of your father — Buckingham flatters Richard's resemblance to Richard Duke of York
Richard's 1482 Scottish campaign, which took Berwick — his actual military record
mute statues — 'statuas' is an old Latinate plural
senior legal officer of the City of London
feign some fear — the stage direction in the dialogue
a prayer-book as prop — Buckingham's explicit stage-managing of the 'pious Richard' tableau
construct a pious elaboration — 'descant' = rhetorical flourish, from the musical term for counterpoint

glosses.phrase: Play the maid’s part, still answer nay, and take it
glosses.definition: act the reluctant maiden — keep saying no but accept. Buckingham's frankest line: the king's pretended refusal is scripted seduction
the lead-covered roof; upper level
lounging on a lascivious couch — Buckingham's specific contrast with Edward IV's libertinism
a pair of prostitutes
two profound theologians — the prop clergymen
at their prayer-beads (the rosary)

glosses.phrase: Two props of virtue for a Christian prince
glosses.definition: two pillars of virtue for a Christian prince — Buckingham's staged acclamation of Richard between the two bishops; the bravura peak of the scene's hypocrisy

glosses.phrase: True ornaments to know a holy man
glosses.definition: the real signs by which a holy man is recognized — Buckingham sells the tableau to the Mayor
the throne of England
sceptred kingship
inheritance by birthright
corrupted lineage — a new euphemism for Edward's 'illegitimate' children

glosses.phrase: royal stock graft with ignoble plants
glosses.definition: royal tree grafted with base shoots — horticultural image for dynastic adulteration
oblivion doubled — the complete erasure of the Yorkist line
remedy, cure
mere agent for another's benefit — Buckingham insists Richard must be king in his own right, not regent
sovereign rule
kingship as a heavy, ornamented burden
formally, this is my answer — Richard's legalistic feint

glosses.phrase: bark to brook no mighty sea
glosses.definition: small boat unfit for the great sea — Richard's humility-performance

glosses.phrase: first he was contract to Lady Lucy
glosses.definition: Buckingham presses the pre-contract claim — legally devastating to Edward IV's marriage's validity

glosses.phrase: Bona, sister to the King of France
glosses.definition: Bona of Savoy — the diplomatic marriage Warwick had been negotiating when Edward secretly wed Elizabeth Woodville

glosses.phrase: care-crazed mother of a many children
glosses.definition: care-worn mother of several children — Buckingham's dismissive portrait of Elizabeth Woodville
late middle age — past her prime
shameful descent and hateful bigamy — the charge that Edward's two 'marriages' made him a bigamist
womanly compassion — Elizabethan gender-coding of mercy as 'soft'
not stony-hearted — Richard's yielding-formula
pierceable by your kind pleas — the language of seductive yielding applied to politics

glosses.phrase: Long live Richard, England’s royal king
glosses.definition: the formal acclamation — Buckingham supplies the line the citizens would not

annotations.title: Buckingham reports failure — and the scene's hinge
annotations.body: The scene opens with Buckingham's account of what just failed: the Guildhall audience refused to acclaim Richard king. Buckingham laid out the entire propaganda package — Edward IV's pre-contract with Lady Lucy, his marriage-by-proxy to Bona of Savoy, his sexual predation on city wives, his own supposed illegitimacy (an extraordinary slander of Richard's own mother), and Richard's personal virtues (Scottish campaign, discipline, bounty) — and was met with "dumb statuas." Only ten voices cried "God save King Richard!" and those were Buckingham's own followers planted at the lower end of the hall. The first conclusion the scene reaches is political: Richard's coup does not have public consent. The second conclusion is theatrical: if consent cannot be won, it must be staged. The remainder of the scene is that staging.
sources: Rosemary Horrox, Richard III: A Study of Service (1989)
Thomas More, History of King Richard III (c. 1513)

annotations.title: Buckingham's stage-directions spoken aloud
annotations.body: Buckingham's instructions to Richard are as frank as theatrical direction can be: intend some fear, be not easily won, play the maid's part still answer nay and take it, get a prayer-book, stand betwixt two churchmen. Shakespeare lets Buckingham narrate the coming scene to Richard before they stage it for the Mayor. The maid's-part line — "Play the maid's part, still answer nay, and take it" — is one of the most corrosive single sentences in English political theatre. The simile's gender is not accidental. The staging of pretended reluctance is explicitly likened to scripted seduction: a woman who says no while yielding, a script designed so that the refusal itself is the invitation. Richard will accept the crown the way (Buckingham says) women accept suitors. Politics and seduction are made grammatically identical.
sources: Madonne M. Miner, "'Neither mother, wife, nor England's queen'" (1980)

annotations.title: "Two props of virtue for a Christian prince"
annotations.body: The tableau's peak: Buckingham points to Richard standing between two bishops with a prayer book in his hand and says "Two props of virtue for a Christian prince, / To stay him from the fall of vanity." The line works three ways simultaneously. First, it is straight propaganda — Buckingham is selling Richard to the Mayor. Second, it is explicit theatre — the audience knows the bishops are props, the book a prop, the piety a performance. Third, it is bitterly ironic — the actual historical Richard III was a deeply pious man by the standards of his period, and Shakespeare's scene nevertheless asks us to read all his piety as performance. The play makes no room for sincere Ricardian religion. What Shakespeare wants us to see is how completely piety can be weaponized as cover, regardless of the underlying state of the actor's soul.
sources: Anne Sutton, "'A curious searcher for our weal public': Richard III, piety, chivalry and the concept of the 'good prince'" (1986)
A. P. Rossiter, Angel with Horns (1961)

annotations.title: Richard's speech of refusal, which is his acceptance
annotations.body: Richard's long refusal-speech (137–169) is rhetorically astonishing. He divides his response into a precise rhetorical quadrant — to speak is ungracious, not to speak looks like tongue-tied acceptance; to reprove is to scold his friends, to accept is unworthy. Having announced the dilemma, he refuses the crown on grounds of personal unworthiness ("my poverty of spirit," "mighty and so many my defects") and on grounds of legitimate succession ("The royal tree hath left us royal fruit" — i.e., the prince should inherit). Both reasons are false. The speech works because each is individually plausible; the combined effect is to make acceptance, when it comes, look like the regrettable overriding of personal scruple by public duty. Shakespeare is dramatizing the specific structure by which illegitimate power claims legitimacy through theatre.
sources: Brian Vickers, The Artistry of Shakespeare's Prose (1968)
A. P. Rossiter, Angel with Horns (1961)
Wolfgang Clemen, A Commentary on Shakespeare's Richard III (1968)

annotations.title: The pre-contract argument and its legal weight
annotations.body: Buckingham's legal argument that Edward IV's marriage to Elizabeth Woodville was invalid — because Edward had been pre-contracted to Lady Lucy and had a marriage-by-proxy to Bona of Savoy, making his subsequent marriage bigamous — is drawn from the historical petition Titulus Regius (January 1484), which formally declared Richard III legitimate king. The pre-contract doctrine (matrimonium per verba de praesenti, followed by intercourse, created indissoluble marriage) was live canon law, and the Eleanor Butler pre-contract (substituted historically for the "Lady Lucy" Shakespeare gives) was the actual legal basis. Historians continue to debate whether any pre-contract actually existed or whether Richard's regime manufactured the evidence. Shakespeare's scene keeps the audience aware that the argument has legal form while making us see its propagandistic function. The two are simultaneously true: the claim is both legally articulated and politically engineered.
sources: R. H. Helmholz, "The Sons of Edward IV" (1986)

annotations.title: Buckingham's ultimatum and Richard's "yielding"
annotations.body: The scene's resolution depends on Buckingham's threat: if Richard will not accept, we will "plant some other in the throne, / To the disgrace and downfall of your house." The threat is the mechanism by which Richard can accept without appearing to have wanted to. Richard's line — "I am not made of stones, / But penetrable to your kind entreats, / Albeit against my conscience and my soul" — completes the scripted seduction Buckingham named earlier: he lets himself be pierced by the request. The final acclamation ("Long live Richard, England's royal king!") follows in a single line. The scene has taken 226 lines to produce a single formal result — a coronation date — but the production has mattered more than the result. The play now gives us a king acclaimed, however theatrically, and the coming scenes will test whether the theatricality can be extended into governance. It cannot.
sources: Robert Weimann, Shakespeare and the Popular Tradition in the Theater (1967)
Emrys Jones, Scenic Form in Shakespeare (1971)

trials.prompt: What happened when Buckingham addressed the citizens at Guildhall?
options: They acclaimed Richard enthusiastically
They responded with silence; only ten voices (Buckingham's own planted followers) cried "God save King Richard!"
They rioted and demanded Edward V's coronation
They demanded Buckingham be king instead

trials.prompt: Buckingham instructs Richard: "Play the maid's part, still answer nay, and take it." The simile compares political acceptance to what?
options: The role of a young actor playing a female part on the Elizabethan stage
Scripted seduction — a woman refusing while yielding, where the refusal itself is the invitation
A child's game of hide and seek
A legal formality used in marriage contracts

trials.prompt: Buckingham's line "Two props of virtue for a Christian prince, / To stay him from the fall of vanity" points to Richard flanked by what, and to what end?
options: Two guards — to suggest prudent caution
Two churchmen, with a prayer-book in his hand — piety as stage prop, legitimizing the claim to the throne
Two family relatives — to suggest dynastic legitimacy
Two scholars — to suggest intellectual depth

trials.prompt: Richard's formal refusal speech (137–169) works how?
options: It is a sincere declaration of unworthiness that Richard genuinely holds
It deploys two individually plausible arguments (personal unworthiness, legitimate succession), combining to make the eventual acceptance look like reluctant overriding of personal scruple
It is incoherent, revealing Richard's cracking composure
It relies on direct biblical citation

trials.prompt: The legal argument that Edward IV's children are illegitimate — based on a pre-contract to "Lady Lucy" and the Bona of Savoy proxy — has what basis?
options: Pure invention; no such arguments were ever made
It draws on the historical Titulus Regius (1484), which formally declared Richard III legitimate king — a real legal argument historians still debate
It was invented by Henry VII after Bosworth to discredit Richard
It originates in Holinshed alone, with no earlier source

trials.prompt: What is the scene's larger claim about political legitimacy?
options: Legitimacy rests on ancient lineage, independent of performance
Illegitimate power claims legitimacy through theatre — the acceptance must look like a reluctant yielding rather than a seizure
Legitimacy requires only military strength
Only God can confer legitimacy, which is why the clergymen are necessary
